title: Provider-neutral financial ledger technical design
content: # Financial Ledger technical design
## Outcome and boundary

LEDGER is Tally's provider-neutral system of record for financial facts and their history. It is a local .NET 10 Native-AOT command-line executable backed by one embedded SQLite store. It records canonical transactions, generic evidence links, reconciliation decisions and coverage, account/category/payment/spend-pool dimensions, financial relationships, and exact actuals. It has no daemon, listener, HTTP surface, hosted dependency, or external database.

Hermes is an external orchestrator. Hermes may read AgentMail, parse MIME, interpret bank wording, request owner clarification, schedule queries, format results, and deliver through WhatsApp. Tally does not poll mailboxes, understand AgentMail or WhatsApp, manage sender or recipient allowlists, acknowledge messages, retain MIME payloads, schedule delivery, or retry delivery. Hermes invokes the same typed public CLI contracts as any other caller. Opaque external references may be stored as evidence metadata; transport payloads and transport semantics may not cross the boundary.

INGEST owns source parsing, preview, explicit approval, immutable manifests, replay protection, batch orchestration, receipts, crash recovery, and source privacy. It may read an approved statement as a source, but it passes only normalized provider-neutral row observations, typed statement facts, and opaque manifest references to LEDGER; neither raw statements nor email payloads become Ledger state. For each row INGEST requests a compatibility projection and applies exactly one closed disposition. The approved statement is authoritative over earlier agent-capture evidence for current financial facts. LEDGER independently validates every disposition, commits each evidence outcome atomically, and owns the durable transaction/evidence/reconciliation history. CLASSIFY proposes one category assignment. BUDGET owns plans and the direct-versus-subtree and overlap rules for stable category references; it compares them with LEDGER's exact pool/category actuals. INSIGHTS consumes structured facts and exceptions. None is a runtime dependency of LEDGER.

The design adapts the reusable intent of CORE and Actions prior art: vertical slices, CQRS-light class separation, explicit dependency injection, CommandResult-style expected outcomes, closed system-owned values, stable errors, and pure mapping. It deliberately does not inherit FastEndpoints, HTTP, Aspire, PostgreSQL, EF Core, DbContext factories, RLS, tenancy, Angular, grid DTOs, seeds, permissions, navigation, menus, translations, frontend key models, or browser tests.
## System and component shape
One process contains six code zones:
1. `Cli` parses explicit resource commands and the versioned envelope, resolves one `OperationDescriptor`, and maps one result to stdout, stderr, and an exit category.

2. `Contracts/Ledger` contains concrete source-generated JSON request, result, error, schema, and enum types. Public contracts never expose SQLite, SQL, paths, provider classes, transport fields, or implementation exceptions.

3. `Features/Ledger` groups typed commands and queries by accounts/dimensions, transactions/evidence, reconciliation, relationships/actuals, and recovery/storage. Commands own validation and transaction boundaries; queries do not mutate Durable Ledger State.
4. `Domain/Ledger` contains money, date, lifecycle, evidence identity, reconciliation policy, relationship, attribution, and actuals policies as pure deterministic code.
5. `Infrastructure/Storage` contains the concrete `Microsoft.Data.Sqlite` connection, SQL, migrations, store-generation management, manifests, and narrow host adapters.

6. `Bootstrap/IntegrationGuidance` contains explicit DI and operation registration plus optional versioned provider guidance. The executable's self-describing schema is sufficient without installing a skill.
The executable may ship beside the maintained native SQLite library. It remains one application process with one owner-private data root and no database server.
## Interface exploration and selected contract
Three public shapes were considered sequentially.
### Alternative A: minimal reconciliation entry points

`reconcile evaluate`, `reconcile apply`, and `reconcile resolve` would minimize command count. They also overload materially different outcomes, obscure coverage/history semantics, and invite open payload dictionaries. The shape was rejected because stable automation needs explicit schemas and stable failure categories.
### Alternative B: statement batch import API

A Ledger-owned preview/commit/status batch would be convenient for INGEST. It would duplicate INGEST's already-approved manifests, approval, replay, receipts, and crash-recovery lifecycle and couple the canonical ledger to statement-import vocabulary. The shape was rejected at the module boundary.
### Alternative C: reconciliation gateway abstraction

An `IReconciliationGateway` could hide internal storage and appear extensible. There is one internal implementation and all external consumers already cross the CLI process boundary. The abstraction would add a hypothetical substitution seam without reducing risk. It was rejected.
### Category hierarchy alternatives

The owner rejected a flat catalogue and retained one category per transaction. Three hierarchy shapes were compared. A materialized path or nested-set model makes reads fast but turns ordinary moves into broad rewrites and weakens append-only audit. A separate generic graph would support arbitrary taxonomies but adds traversal and cycle complexity that no second use case needs. The selected adjacency history gives each category zero or one current parent, rejects cycles transactionally, permits assignment to any active node, and derives exact direct or subtree roll-ups. This is the smallest interface that preserves stable identity, attributable moves, and deterministic reporting.
### Selected hybrid
LEDGER exposes explicit resource operations from one registry and keeps the domain contract deep:
- register provider-neutral evidence and link non-confirming supporting evidence;
- query a compatibility projection without mutation;

- apply one closed reconciliation disposition: `match_existing`, `correct_existing_from_statement`, `create_statement_only`, `record_ambiguous`, or `record_exception`; create and correct dispositions carry typed authoritative statement facts rather than an open source payload;
- inspect, owner-confirm, reject, revoke, or replace decisions;
- complete and inspect statement-scope coverage;
- manage stable local payment-instrument, cardholder, and spend-pool identifiers and append attribution/assignment corrections.

An apply command is atomic for one evidence record, not an INGEST batch. It re-evaluates mutable preconditions inside the writer transaction rather than trusting a preview token. This preserves Ledger safety while leaving INGEST in control of batch orchestration.
## Public command contract
The explicit CLI path and stable operation ID are generated from one registry. No generic invoke, save, update, delete, SQL, database, metadata-bag, or batch command is public. No aliases ship in v1.
| CLI family | Stable operation IDs |
| `tally ledger account` | `ledger.account.create`, `get`, `list`, `rename`, `archive` |
| `tally ledger category` | `ledger.category.create`, `get`, `list`, `rename`, `reparent`, `archive`, `reactivate` |
| `tally ledger instrument` | `ledger.instrument.create`, `get`, `list`, `rename`, `archive`, `reactivate` |
| `tally ledger cardholder` | `ledger.cardholder.create`, `get`, `list`, `rename`, `archive`, `reactivate` |
| `tally ledger pool` | `ledger.pool.create`, `get`, `list`, `rename`, `archive`, `reactivate` |
| `tally ledger transaction` | `ledger.transaction.record`, `get`, `void`, `supersede` |
| `tally ledger transaction category` | `ledger.transaction.category.assign`, `correct` |
| `tally ledger transaction attribution` | `ledger.transaction.attribution.assign`, `correct` |
| `tally ledger transaction pool` | `ledger.transaction.pool.assign`, `correct` |
| `tally ledger evidence` | `ledger.evidence.register`, `get`, `link-supporting` |
| `tally ledger reconciliation` | `ledger.reconciliation.candidates`, `apply` |
| `tally ledger reconciliation decision` | `ledger.reconciliation.decision.get`, `confirm`, `reject`, `revoke`, `replace` |
| `tally ledger reconciliation coverage` | `ledger.reconciliation.coverage.complete`, `get` |
| `tally ledger transfer` | `ledger.transfer.confirm`, `revoke`, `replace` |
| `tally ledger refund` | `ledger.refund.confirm`, `revoke`, `replace` |
| `tally ledger relationship` | `ledger.relationship.get` |
| `tally ledger actuals` | `ledger.actuals.query` |
| `tally ledger backup` | `ledger.backup.create`, `verify` |
| `tally ledger restore` | `ledger.restore.prepare`, `activate` |
| `tally ledger storage` | `ledger.storage.status`, `evolution.prepare`, `evolution.activate` |
| `tally schema` | `system.schema.list`, `system.schema.show` |
| `tally version` | `system.version` |
| `tally guidance` | `system.guidance.list`, `check`, `install` |

Every operation has one concrete input and result schema and a closed stable error set. Unknown fields are rejected. Mutations and complex queries use explicit JSON input via `--input -` or `--input @file`; the marker prevents an absent pipe from blocking. Small read selectors may use named options. Mutations require `--idempotency-key`. The envelope contains `contractVersion`, `operationId`, actor context, and input. Actor context is attribution rather than application authentication; the runtime separately records trusted OS identity and time.

Money enters and leaves as a canonical decimal string such as `-123.45`; storage uses signed `Int64` minor units. Dates are `yyyy-MM-dd`. JSON numeric money, exponent notation, excessive scale, negative zero, overflow, and unknown fields fail before storage access.

Each invocation writes exactly one envelope to stdout. Success includes contract version, operation ID, `outcome=success`, and a stable result. Expected failure includes `outcome=error`, stable code, category, safe message, and optional field metadata. Diagnostics use stderr and safe metadata plus a correlation reference; descriptions, finance payloads, unmasked identifiers, request/response serialization, credentials, and stack traces are prohibited. Exit categories are: 0 success; 2 CLI/schema usage; 3 validation; 4 not found; 5 conflict or busy; 6 invalid lifecycle, relationship, attribution, or reconciliation transition; 7 contract, guidance, cursor, policy, or storage-version incompatibility; 8 integrity, ambiguity, review-required, backup, candidate, or activation failure; 9 permission, disk, or host-resource failure; 10 sanitized unexpected failure. The structured error code is authoritative.
## Durable model and invariants
### Canonical transactions and evidence

`TransactionFact` contains only immutable canonical finance facts: transaction ULID, account ULID, signed minor units, currency, transaction date, optional distinct posting date, and owner-safe description. It does not contain a transport-specific provenance enum or a single source reference.

An `EvidenceRecord` has a local ULID, a closed provider-neutral kind (`agent_capture`, `statement_row`, `receipt`, `external_document`, or `owner_assertion`), a privacy-safe logical identity digest, optional opaque external reference, optional content fingerprint, actor/time, and an allowlisted `EvidenceObservation`. The observation may contain account, signed amount, currency, transaction/posting dates, stable local instrument/cardholder references, and a description fingerprint. Raw mailbox, MIME, chat, statement-file, and provider payloads are never stored.

Evidence registration is idempotent at two levels. The mutation envelope prevents replay of the same request, while the unique logical identity prevents the same real evidence from being registered through another request key. A changed payload for either identity is a conflict. A record can link to multiple canonical transactions only where the declared link role permits it; confirming statement evidence actively confirms at most one current transaction. Supporting links are not reconciliation decisions. Every confirming link is created or changed only with an append-only decision.
### Reconciliation projection, decisions, and states

Candidate projection is read-only. It returns compatible active unreconciled transactions, rejection reasons, current evidence conflicts, an opaque projection token, and the policy identifier/version. The token is advisory and cannot authorize a write.

`ledger.reconciliation.apply` receives the evidence ID, expected observation fingerprint, selected disposition, selected or complete candidate set, typed authoritative statement facts for create or correct, policy ID/version or explicit owner authority, exact match basis, and reason. Inside one `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction LEDGER revalidates evidence identity, economic-event compatibility, current facts and history, uniqueness, links, and disposition-specific rules. `match_existing` appends a confirming decision and link without a new fact. `correct_existing_from_statement` creates an immutable statement-derived replacement and appends supersession, decision, confirming link, explicit category/pool carry-forward, compatible payment carry-forward or unknown initialization, and any invariant-preserving transfer/refund relationship replacement. If an active relationship would become invalid, correction is review-required and applies no financial effect. The original agent-capture fact remains historical and exactly one replacement is active. `create_statement_only` creates the fact with explicit unknown-payment and unassigned-pool initialization. Every effect and its idempotency outcome commits together; a crash leaves all or none.
The externally visible current states are:
- `recorded_unreconciled`;
- `statement_reconciled`;
- `statement_only`;
- `recorded_absent_from_statement`;
- `ambiguous_match`;
- `owner_confirmed_match`;
- `reconciliation_exception`.

They are derived from immutable facts, active replacements, decisions, evidence links, and statement coverage rather than overwritten flags. Statement-reconciled detail distinguishes a confirmed existing fact from a fact corrected from statement. Decision history records whether an outcome was deterministic or owner-approved, the statement authority basis, reason, actor, time, policy ID/version, exact match basis, predecessor and replacement facts, dimension carry-forward or unknown initialization, relationship replacement events, previous decision, and revoke/replace history. Confirm, reject, revoke, and replace are separately idempotent operations. No ambiguous candidate is applied automatically.

An automatic policy is compiled and versioned, not runtime-configurable. A conceptual strong match requires compatible account, currency, signed amount, transaction date, one unique active unreconciled candidate, and no conflicting evidence. A broader guard-candidate rule must prevent unsafe correction or statement-only creation when a plausible shifted-date or otherwise near candidate exists. OQ-LEDGER-13 remains an evidence gate: until private paired agent-capture and statement evidence validates bounded fields, tolerances, and conflict rules, projection may report candidates but automatic match, correction, and statement-only application remain disabled. Explicit owner-approved correction remains available with reason and full audit history. The final automatic subset must persist its policy version and exact basis.

A `StatementScope` identifies one approved coverage period and account without containing provider payloads. Completing coverage is idempotent and validates that every statement-row evidence outcome is durable. It derives unmatched prior transactions as `recorded_absent_from_statement`, preserves ambiguous and other exceptions, and returns exact counts plus an exception list. Reopening or correcting coverage requires an append-only replacement decision; rows are never silently discarded.
### Independent financial dimensions

Account, payment instrument, cardholder, spend pool, and spend category are separate catalogues with stable local ULIDs and attributable append-only lifecycle events. Spend Categories form an acyclic hierarchy: each node has zero or one current parent, sibling names are unique, moves append parent history, and any active node can receive the transaction's sole active Category Assignment. Exact category queries distinguish direct membership from current-hierarchy subtree roll-up and never duplicate all-up totals. Instruments and cardholders may have owner labels, an account association where known, and an optional masked suffix; full provider account/card identifiers are prohibited. Tally IDs are authoritative. Hermes may map provider wording to them but Ledger never interprets that wording.

Each transaction is atomically initialized with explicit current attribution for instrument and cardholder, each independently known or unknown. Attribution is append-only and correctable. It is not inferred from account, category, evidence kind, or description.

Each transaction is atomically initialized with an explicit `unassigned` pool state and, under the v1 assumption, has at most one active pool assignment. Assignment and correction append history and never change the transaction fact. OQ-LEDGER-15 remains a pre-implementation evidence gate: if representative mixed purchases require split pools, the PRD and this model must be revised before implementation. No pool is inferred from account, instrument, cardholder, or category.

Ordinary supersession creates a new fact and retires the old fact through history; evidence, category, pool, instrument, and cardholder state do not silently move. Statement-authoritative reconciliation is the one composite correction: it explicitly records the replacement, supersession, decision, confirming link, category/pool carry-forward, compatible payment carry-forward or unknown initialization, and any invariant-preserving transfer/refund relationship replacement atomically. An incompatible active relationship blocks the correction for review. The statement therefore governs active facts without erasing capture history or transiently corrupting spend semantics. Refunds follow the original current category and pool unless explicitly corrected. Owned-account transfer principal contributes zero External Spend and Budget Actual regardless of dimension assignment. Separately recorded bank fees remain spend. OQ-LEDGER-16 alone gates whether a cash withdrawal is immediate spend or a transfer to tracked cash.
### Financial relationships and actuals

Signed amount is the owner's economic-position delta: asset increases and liability reductions are positive; asset decreases and liability increases are negative. ZAR uses exact signed `Int64` cents. Effective Date is transaction date; a distinct supplied posting date is preserved.

Transfers require two active distinct-account transactions with equal-and-opposite principal and explicit outflow/inflow roles. Refunds link one active original outflow to one active same-account refund credit in ZAR only when their absolute minor-unit amounts are exactly equal. Existing role exclusivity rejects a second active refund or any transfer/refund role overlap; partial and over-refund relationships are unsupported. A full refund may make later-period net spend negative. Net Account Movement is the signed selected sum. External Spend is qualifying outflow magnitude minus active in-set refund magnitude, excluding transfer principal and unrelated inflows. Budget Actual applies the same formula to each pool/category cell, including explicit `unassigned` and `uncategorized` buckets. Exact full-set totals equal the sum of returned dimension groups. BUDGET owns plans and performance decisions; LEDGER only returns facts and exact actuals.

Actuals filters may include account, category with `exact` or `subtree` scope, pool, instrument, cardholder, evidence kind, reconciliation state, relationship type, and date range. Grouping supports pool totals, category-direct totals, category-subtree totals, and the pool-by-category matrix without conflating dimensions or double counting. Reconciliation normally changes evidence and state only; the explicit statement-authoritative replacement disposition may change current movement or spend, and then the superseded fact contributes zero while the active replacement contributes exactly once.
## Application flows
### Ordinary mutation

CLI adapter -> descriptor/request mapper -> typed command handler -> concrete `LedgerDb` write transaction -> pure policy -> SQL facts/history/idempotency -> commit -> stable result -> process envelope.

Boundary validation occurs before the idempotency key is consumed. Domain mutation, attributable history, evidence and decision effects, and `IdempotencyRecord` commit together. Expected errors return a result rather than throw. `CancellationToken` flows through each asynchronous boundary.
### Match-first statement reconciliation

INGEST approved normalized row -> `evidence.register` -> `reconciliation.candidates` -> INGEST selects a permitted disposition -> `reconciliation.apply` -> LEDGER transactional revalidation and commit -> structured outcome. Exact matches confirm the existing fact. A correction carries authoritative statement facts separately from evidence metadata and atomically replaces the active notification-derived fact with full history, explicit dimension carry-forward or unknown initialization, and any still-valid relationship replacement; an incompatible relationship blocks the correction for review. A no-match row may create one statement-only fact. Ambiguity carries the complete candidate set and returns a durable review item or stable review-required outcome; it is never guessed. At batch end INGEST calls `coverage.complete`, receives exact counts and exceptions, and incorporates them into its own immutable receipt. Raw statement and email payloads remain outside LEDGER.
### Query and snapshot flow

Ordinary get/list queries use a read-only SQLite connection and never mutate durable state. The first actuals page is different only because it creates an ephemeral query cache. After boundary validation it acquires the single-writer lock and begins one immediate transaction. That transaction reads a coherent active projection across facts, category/pool/payment attribution, evidence, reconciliation, coverage, and relationships; computes exact full-set totals and groups; materializes `QuerySnapshot` and ordered items; and commits before returning a cursor. A busy store returns a stable busy result. A crash or cancellation before commit exposes no cursor and no partial snapshot.

Later pages contain only the cursor and use read-only connections. Cursor version, contract version, filter hash, generation fingerprint, and expiry are validated. Snapshot tables are derived ephemeral cache, cleaned opportunistically, excluded from backup and Durable Ledger State, and require no daemon. Snapshot creation consumes no mutation idempotency key because it changes no durable domain state.
### Backup, restore, and evolution

Backup creation acquires the writer lock, makes a consistent online snapshot into private staging, validates integrity, foreign keys, schema, counts, category hierarchy acyclicity and ancestry, exact direct/subtree/pool totals, replacement, dimension carry-forward or unknown initialization, relationship-replacement chains and conservation, evidence uniqueness, statement-authority decision/link state, coverage, dimension cardinality, and idempotency outcomes, writes a checksum manifest, flushes, and atomically publishes. Verification repeats the checks independently and never mutates the ledger.

Restore and storage evolution are two-stage. Prepare builds a separate generation, restores or migrates it, and returns a candidate plus immutable verification fingerprint. Activate requires explicit replacement authorization and the expected current fingerprint; it revalidates under the exclusive lock and atomically replaces the owner-only `CURRENT` pointer. The old generation remains a recovery point. Failure before pointer replacement leaves it current; after replacement the verified candidate is current. Startup rejects missing, malformed, or unverified generations and reports a recovery action without guessing.
## Persistence, privacy, and concurrency

The private data root contains generation directories, one `CURRENT` pointer, retained recovery generations, and manifests. The selected linux-x64 host contract requires a 0700 root and 0600 database, WAL, SHM, candidate, manifest, and backup artifacts. The root must reside on a host-encrypted volume with OS-managed keys. Stock SQLite encryption is not claimed and Tally contains no custom cryptography. Production use requires verifiable cloud-provider encryption evidence or a separately encrypted data volume.

SQLite uses `foreign_keys=ON`, WAL, a bounded busy timeout, and `synchronous=FULL` for durable finance writes. `user_version` selects ordered immutable migrations. One exclusive writer is the supported concurrency model; a second writer receives a stable busy/conflict result. Raw SQLite identifiers use stable snake_case. SQL constraints and transactional revalidation enforce invariants; pure policy code produces stable reasons and match bases.

Provider-neutral privacy is structural: contract schemas use closed allowlists; evidence cannot accept an arbitrary metadata object; public models and diagnostics prohibit provider payloads and transport identifiers; persisted external references are opaque and optional; description fingerprints are distinct from display descriptions. A schema-and-diagnostic canary test prevents AgentMail, MIME, WhatsApp, mailbox, sender, recipient, delivery, and retry concepts from entering public LEDGER contracts.
## Interface and mapping economics

The only general application interfaces are `ICommandHandler<TCommand,TResult>` and `IQueryHandler<TQuery,TResult>`. Destructive platform seams are `IAuthoritativeStoreActivator` and `IHostArtifactProtection`. `TimeProvider` is used directly. `LedgerDb`, SQLite connections, SQL, serialization, hashes, IDs, calculators, match policy, `ContractMapper`, and `RowMapper` remain concrete. There is no generic repository, provider-neutral unit of work, mediator, event bus, file-system abstraction, generic mapper interface, reconciliation gateway, or migration interface until a second implementation creates a real substitution need.

Boundary mappers convert public contracts to domain values without database access. Row mappers are pure concrete transformers. Commands, not mappers, own business errors, transactions, and orchestration. This preserves the useful CORE mapper intent without copying an EF upsert model.
## Feature boundaries

- **Contract and Integration Guidance:** operation registry, versioned schemas, help, process behavior, compatibility, and optional provider guidance. Public schema is authoritative; installed guidance is never required.
- **Accounts and Financial Dimensions:** account, hierarchical category, instrument, cardholder, and spend-pool catalogues plus attributable lifecycle and category-parent history.
- **Transactions, Evidence, and Attribution:** immutable facts, transaction corrections, generic evidence, category assignment, payment attribution, and pool assignment.
- **Evidence Reconciliation and Coverage:** candidate projection, atomic dispositions, decision history, explicit owner resolution, statement scopes, coverage, and exceptions.
- **Relationships and Actuals:** transfer/refund lifecycle, exact classifications, filters, pool/category matrix, and snapshots.
- **Recovery and Storage:** raw SQLite, permissions, backups, restore, immutable migrations, generation validation, and atomic activation.
## CORE and full-stack compliance matrix
| Design axis | LEDGER disposition |
| Save/upsert | Rejected. Create, rename, archive/reactivate, void/supersede, assign/correct, register/link, apply, confirm/reject/revoke/replace, complete, and activate are narrow operations. |
| Get detail/list | Explicit get/list operations return stable detail and history projections. |
| Grid/lookup | No UI or grid. Bounded catalogue lists and paged actuals are channel-neutral contracts, not frontend DTOs. |
| Delete/bulk delete | Omitted. Financial state uses archive, void, supersede, reject, revoke, and replace. |
| Domain endpoints | Represented as typed CLI operations, not HTTP endpoints. No Lex endpoint entity is appropriate. |
| DTOs/frontend key models | Concrete source-generated CLI contracts exist. Frontend models belong to external consumers, including Hermes-owned delivery surfaces. |
| Commands/queries | One explicit handler per operation. Queries are read-only except excluded atomic snapshot cache creation. |
| Mapping | Pure concrete `ContractMapper` and `RowMapper`; no EF change tracking or generic mapper interface. |
| EF/DbContext | Not applicable because raw SQLite is deliberate. `LedgerDb` owns explicit caller-scoped connections and transactions. |
| Expected paths | Greenfield locations are listed below. Lex 0.5.4 validates only existing paths, so expected-path metadata is deferred until the first scaffold task creates them. |
| Auth/permissions/seeds | Not applicable inside the single-owner OS trust boundary. No users, tenants, roles, permissions, or finance seed data. |
| Navigation/translations/browser fixtures | Not applicable. LEDGER has no human delivery surface. |
| UI ownership | Hermes and other external consumers own presentation, schedules, recipient disclosure, throttling, and transport. No mockups, routes, components, or browser tests exist in LEDGER. |
| Provider integrations | Explicitly prohibited. Provider wording is resolved outside Tally to stable local IDs and provider-neutral evidence. |

Inherited prior art is applied selectively. `ADR-CORE-0010`, `ADR-CORE-0021`, `ADR-CORE-0024`, `ADR-CORE-0025`, `ADR-CORE-0004`, and `ADR-CORE-0020` supply reusable intent. Pure mapper intent from `ADR-CORE-0022` is adapted. PascalCase database naming, EF configuration, DbContext factories, FastEndpoints, Aspire, PostgreSQL, RLS, tenancy, and UI patterns are not applicable or are rejected by the LEDGER boundary. `ADR-CORE-0030` and `ARCH-CORE-TALLY-SYSTEM-CONTEXT` govern the Hermes/Tally boundary.
## Test architecture

- Pure unit tests cover money, dates, lifecycle machines, category hierarchy cycles and roll-ups, evidence identity, reconciliation and source-authority policy matrices, decision transitions, replacement, dimension carry-forward or unknown initialization, relationship-replacement rules, coverage, dimension cardinality, transfer/refund conservation, and exact pool/category actuals.

- Handler integration tests use real temporary SQLite stores for constraints, transaction atomicity, logical and request idempotency, reconciliation crash points, snapshots, backup, restore, and migrations. SQL behavior is not mocked.

- Published-binary process tests validate every descriptor, schema generation, JSON stdin, stdout/stderr isolation, stable errors, exits, offline behavior, provider-neutral schemas, and no orphan process.

- Property tests generate correction, authoritative statement replacement, refund, reversal, transfer, category hierarchy/move, pool, and payment histories and prove one-active-effect, acyclic hierarchy, exact movement/spend, and group-total invariants.

- Recovery tests inject failure before and after database commit, artifact publication, candidate verification, and `CURRENT` replacement. Only activation and host-protection ports are substituted; supported-host tests exercise real permissions and atomic rename.

- Security tests validate 0700/0600 artifacts and scan schema, diagnostics, errors, manifests, and evidence rows for provider/transport vocabulary, raw payloads, descriptions, unmasked identifiers, credentials, and request serialization.

- Performance tests use 100000 active transactions with representative evidence and dimensions, enforcing first-page/full actuals under 2 seconds p95 and ordinary writes under 1 second p95 on the benchmark host.
- Recovery drills compare every Durable Ledger State type, exact totals, relationships, evidence, decisions, coverage, dimensions, history, and idempotency outcomes.
## Planned implementation locations
The repository is intentionally documentation-first and greenfield. These are implementation obligations, not present files:
- `src/Tally/Tally.csproj`
- `src/Tally/Cli`
- `src/Tally/Contracts/Ledger`
- `src/Tally/Features/Ledger/{Accounts,Dimensions,Transactions,Evidence,Reconciliation,Relationships,Actuals,Recovery}`
- `src/Tally/Domain/Ledger`
- `src/Tally/Infrastructure/Storage`
- `src/Tally/Bootstrap`
- `src/Tally/IntegrationGuidance`
- `tests/Tally.Tests`
- `tests/Tally.ProcessTests`

Lex 0.5.4 decision path checking treats truthful future paths as missing now. Expected-path metadata therefore remains empty until `lex:plan` creates the first scaffold task. The plan must attach actual paths to decisions once the scaffold exists; fake placeholder code is prohibited.
## Owner decisions and remaining evidence gates

Direct owner policy on 2026-07-21 resolved `OQ-LEDGER-5` for owned-account transfers, linked refunds or reversals, and separately recorded bank fees, and resolved `OQ-LEDGER-6` by requiring hierarchical categories with one active category assignment per transaction. `A5` is validated and the flat-catalogue `A10` is invalidated.

Direct owner correction on 2026-07-22 establishes a full-refund-only model: an eligible refund credit must exactly equal the original outflow magnitude in ZAR on the same account, only one active refund relationship may use the original, both source transactions remain immutable, and partial, cumulative, and over-refund relationships are rejected. `DD-LEDGER-FULL-AMOUNT-REFUND-RELATIONSHIP` governs the contract and confirms that the existing active-role exclusivity trigger remains correct.
| Gate | Required evidence | Affected design |

| `OQ-LEDGER-13` | Paired private agent-capture and statement evidence proving safe automatic identity fields, tolerances, uniqueness, and conflict guards | Automatic match, correction, and statement-only policy; statement authority itself is settled |

| `OQ-LEDGER-15` | Representative company-paid, personal-after-tax, refund, reversal, correction, and mixed-purchase cases | One-active-pool cardinality; category choice does not resolve pool ownership |
| `OQ-LEDGER-16` | Owner choice between immediate cash spend and transfer to a tracked cash account | Cash-withdrawal External Spend and Budget Actual treatment |

Payment-instrument/cardholder identity, category hierarchy, statement-over-capture authority, and transfer zero-spend semantics are design facts. None of the remaining gates permits fail-open inference; unresolved cases remain explicit and reviewable.
origin: LEDGER PRD v0.7; ADR-CORE-0030; ARCH-CORE-TALLY-SYSTEM-CONTEXT; existing LEDGER design decisions; private statement findings through 2026-07-20
